creator: gerard_seibert@outlook.com
text:
(In reply to Bjoern A. Zeeb from comment #211)
The video does point out some fascinating points. It also helps to highlight the fallacy of some of FreeBSD’s embedded methods of operation.

The refusal to use GPL or other licensed code is ridiculous. It reminds me of a man who falls off a boat and then refuses to use a life preserver thrown at him because it was made in China, or it was the wrong color, or whatever. If code is available that works, then use the dam thing. Worry about modifying it at a later date. Would you let millions of people die because your favorite pharmaceutical company did not manufacture the vaccine that could cure a disease? If so, then someone has a severe problem.

FreeBSD’s long-held reputation for failure to support modern standards is well known. I work in a municipality. I could never in good conscience recommend using FreeBSD in our environment. Most of our equipment is less than five years old, which means it probably is not fully supported by FreeBSD. I know for a fact that at least five units here employ Thunderbolt. I have not checked all of the units, but I would be surprised to find that few are using that technology, if not all.

This is not the first time FreeBSD has fallen short of the mark. Hardware support is seriously lacking in the FreeBSD world. Perhaps before they push out a new version, they should first sit back and concentrate on fixing all the broken hardware issues.

Even if they did fix the Thunderbolt issue, they would never backport it. They would hold it for ransom until they released another version of FreeBSD. Thank GOD Microsoft and other OS systems treat their users with more respect.
